title: Fitting tap aerators and flow restrictors
subtitle: Reduce kitchen and bathroom tap flow from 15 litres per minute to 6 without noticing
excerpt: An unmodified UK kitchen tap delivers 12-18 litres per minute. An aerator introduces air into the stream, maintaining perceived pressure while reducing actual flow to 6-8 litres per minute. Flow restrictors in the flexible hose lines reduce it further. Fitting both to a two-tap basin or sink saves 40-60% of tap water with no change in washing experience.
sourceNotes: Waterwise tap flow rate data for UK residential fittings. WRAP water efficiency guidance for household fittings.

content.text: Tap aerators work by mixing air into the water stream through a mesh screen at the tap spout. The mixed stream feels the same pressure as an unaerated stream but uses 40-50% less water. Flow restrictors are disc inserts that fit inside the flexible hose connection at the tap valve and limit the maximum flow rate regardless of how far the tap is opened.
Step 1, check the tap spout thread.

Most UK monobloc and pillar taps have a M22 or M24 threaded spout that accepts a standard aerator insert. Look at the existing spout tip, if there is a knurled ring or hex-shaped tip, it already has an aerator fitting. Unscrew it (anticlockwise) to check the thread size.

Screw the aerator insert onto the spout thread. Hand-tight is sufficient, do not use a wrench on a chrome spout or it will mark the finish. Run the tap to check the stream is aerated (it will appear white and foamy rather than clear).
Step 3, turn off the water supply.
Close the isolation valve on the hot and cold supply hoses below the tap (turn the screw slot perpendicular to the pipe). Open the tap to release residual pressure.

Unscrew the flexible hose from the tap valve. Drop the restrictor disc (rated at the desired flow: 4, 6, or 8 litres per minute) into the female fitting before reconnecting the hose. Reconnect and tighten. Repeat on both hot and cold hoses. Open the isolation valves and check for leaks at the hose connections.
Outdoor taps used to fill water butts or run a pressure washer. The slower flow only saves water if the tap runs to drain. Here it is just a nuisance.
Taps that feed a washing machine or dishwasher. The appliance controls its own water use, so a restrictor changes nothing.
